concerns.text: The validation design is circular/tautological. FR-004 and SC-001 compare the CNN (predicting from raw pixels) against a 'linear regression baseline on hand-crafted grain size features'. In materials science, 'grain size' is a morphological feature EXTRACTED from the microstructure image. If the CNN is trained on the same images, it implicitly learns to extract grain size (and other features) to minimize loss. Comparing a model that learns features from pixels against a model that uses a specific feature (grain size) derived from those same pixels is not an independent validation; it is a test of whether the CNN can replicate a specific feature extraction. The 'validation target' (grain size) is a deterministic function of the predictor's input (image), making the comparison trivial if the CNN learns the physics correctly. A valid baseline must be independent of the image content (e.g., a constant mean predictor, or a physics simulation based on composition, not image-derived morphology).

The 'physics-based baseline' is misdefined. The spec claims to test if 'microstructure images contain sufficient signal' by comparing against a baseline using 'hand-crafted grain size features'. However, grain size is NOT a physics-based simulation; it is a statistical descriptor of the image. A true physics-based baseline would be a Hall-Petch calculation using composition and processing history (if available) or a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) simulation. Using a hand-crafted image feature as the 'physics baseline' conflates 'feature engineering' with 'physics modeling', rendering the hypothesis test ('can we bypass physics-based simulations?') invalid because the baseline IS an image-based feature engineering approach.

The statistical test (paired t-test) assumes the errors of the two models are paired and independent. However, if the baseline (grain size regression) and the CNN are both trained on the exact same image-derived features (grain size is extracted from the image, CNN sees the image), their errors will be highly correlated and potentially non-independent in a way that violates standard t-test assumptions for model comparison. Furthermore, if the baseline is a deterministic function of the image (grain size), the 'paired' nature is trivial. The methodology for statistical validation is unsound for this specific comparison design.

The assumption 'The physics-based baseline (linear regression on hand-crafted grain size features) is a valid reference point' is scientifically inaccurate. Linear regression on grain size is a statistical model, not a physics-based simulation. This misclassification undermines the scientific framing of the research question.
